# Questions in Livestream 126

Live Stream #126: Apocalypse Of The Sock Puppet

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=523" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:43</a> | In 1 Samuel 18:27, why did David give Saul Philistine foreskins, and what context is modern readers missing? | He says this was proof that David had killed the required Philistines, using a body part that could be counted. | He explains the request as an ancient proof-of-fatality practice. Armies often counted a body part after battle to tally the dead; Egyptians, for example, could count right hands. Since Philistines were not circumcised, foreskins served as the countable proof in this episode and were easier to transport than larger body parts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:12</a> | What is his opinion of John Walton's background in Hebrew Bible, the ancient Near East, and languages? | He thinks Walton is an excellent scholar whose work he generally likes, though he has disagreements. | He says he is a fan of Walton and generally agrees with Walton's contextualization of the early biblical books. His reservations are limited rather than wholesale: he mentions disagreement with Walton on demons and on the serpent in the garden, but still treats Walton's broader scholarship as strong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:46</a> | As a sci-fi fan, what does he think of Grand Admiral Thrawn's return to Star Wars? | He says he knows the character but had not watched enough of the new material to give a strong verdict. | He knows Thrawn and likes the character, and he also likes Ahsoka Tano, but he had not watched the Ahsoka series. His comments are therefore framed as a cautious outside impression: he had heard bad things, thought the new Disney-era handling sounded weak, and said that made him less interested in watching. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=1049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:29</a> | In Judges 19:1-25, why did the Levite force his two women out, and what is the cultural context for offering daughters to protect a guest? | He treats the passage as horrifying and explains it through ancient hospitality, social disorder, and the text's own moral indictment. | He stresses that the concubine relationship is coercive, probably rooted in debt and purchase, and that the Levite is already acting selfishly before the crisis. The lodging scene is also framed by shame and hospitality expectations: the men of Gibeah fail hospitality, the host tries to protect the guest even by offering women, and the whole episode illustrates how badly Israel's moral order has collapsed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=1869" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:09</a> | In Judges 19:29, did the Levite lie to Israel by sending pieces of the woman and making it seem like the Benjaminites killed her? | He says yes, the act was a manipulative way to spark outrage. | He reads the text as saying the woman was unresponsive but not yet dead when the Levite carried her away, so the Levite himself killed and dismembered her. The Benjaminites had brutalized her, but the Levite used the body parts to provoke Israel, shift blame, cover his shame, and extract revenge while disappearing from the story afterward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=2225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:05</a> | Why does God not let Israel win immediately in Judges 20:20-28, what is the larger meaning, and what is his favorite Psalm? | He treats the passage as judgment on morally compromised Israel, and he names Psalm 1 as his favorite. | He says Israel presumed the war before properly asking whether they should fight; their first question was essentially which tribe should go first. Because the Levite, Benjamin, and the rest of Israel are all morally compromised, God does not hand them an easy victory. Only after weeping, fasting, sacrifice, and a fuller inquiry does God promise deliverance, and even then the episode remains a grim judgment on all Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=2602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:22</a> | How did the Levites get judged in the same way in the Judges 19-20 episode? | He says the Levites were part of all Israel and shared in the losses and judgment. | He points to Judges 20:2, where all the tribes of Israel are assembled, and says the Levites were included among those present. His answer keeps the larger moral frame of the passage: Benjamin, the Levite, and the wider Israelite assembly are all implicated, so the violence and loss fall across Israel rather than only on one party. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=2780" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:20</a> | Why did God kill David's son, and why was the child part of the judgment against David's sin? | He says the judgment had to wound David and works through the episode as serious covenant discipline. | He ties the judgment to David's murder of Uriah and abuse of power, arguing that murder requires a blood judgment and that a king could not realistically execute that sentence on himself. The child's death is not presented as the child being personally guilty; rather, David is made to pay a direct and painful price, even though God later redeems the situation through Bathsheba bearing Solomon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=3115" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:55</a> | What specific updates would Kenneth Kitchen's book on the reliability of the Old Testament need? | He says most of the book is still useful, but many discoveries since publication would need to be added. | He says Kitchen's book is not obsolete in the sense of being useless, but since its publication many discoveries and much new scholarship have accumulated. The update would mostly be expansion: he imagines the subject now requiring hundreds of additional pages and mentions newer items such as the Dur-Katlimmu ostraca as the sort of material that would need inclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=3270" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:30</a> | What is his take on John Walton's view of Genesis 1 as a cosmic temple dedication text? | He largely agrees with Walton on Genesis 1 as sacred-space and temple language, while disagreeing on the serpent. | He agrees that Genesis 1 functions as a cosmic temple dedication text and as the setup of sacred space where God fills and walks with humanity. He also notes that Genesis 1-3 is complex and that Genesis 1 has a genealogical aspect, but he parts ways with Walton on the serpent, rejecting a chaos-monster reading and preferring a cherubic interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=3411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:51</a> | What happened to his project on the plagues of Egypt? | It has not been abandoned, but time and funding limits have delayed it. | He says the project is still intended and even has a book proposal in progress, but writing a full book requires time he does not currently have while maintaining both a day job and YouTube work. He connects the delay to the channel's funding goals: the project needs enough support to free up sustained writing time, unlike a smaller article that is already difficult to complete under current constraints. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=3751" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:31</a> | What does Ecclesiastes 12:12 mean, and is it condemning great study? | No. He says it warns that excessive study is wearying without condemning scholarship. | He reads the passage in context with the preacher as a wise teacher who has sought and arranged knowledge, so it cannot be an anti-intellectual condemnation. The warning is that endless books and intense devotion to study wear out the body; scholarship is good, but it needs rest, Sabbath, and ordinary ways of recovering from mental fatigue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=4162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:22</a> | What did obelisks represent in Egyptian culture and religion, and do they imitate alien technology? | Obelisks are tied to sun worship and temple symbolism; he rejects the alien-technology idea. | He describes an obelisk as a pyramid-like point on a pillar and as an icon of Egyptian solar worship. He connects early examples to Fifth Dynasty sun temples, where shadows could be traced for cultic coordination, and says they are impressive Bronze Age stone-moving achievements but not technology, much less alien technology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=4349" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:29</a> | How accurate are ancient population estimates for Israel? | He says they are still estimates with the same basic uncertainty problems that affect other ancient demographic reconstructions. | He cautions that the numbers in Numbers may be clan or military-group counts rather than literal thousands, and he notes that this remains controversial even in major Hebrew lexicons. Archaeology still shows Israelite impact, such as four-room houses and food-remain shifts in the highlands, but he thinks the early conquest population was likely in the tens of thousands and admits the exact reading remains unsettled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=4637" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:17</a> | Why does he identify Amraphel with Hammurabi despite the initial guttural, final lamed, and alternative proposals? | He says the sound changes are plausible, while the alternative identifications have worse linguistic or chronological problems. | He argues that guttural interchange can explain the initial sound difference and that Hebrew often avoids final vowels by adding a consonant, which could explain the final lamed. He prefers the Hammurabi identification because the other proposals do not fit the chronology well and can require consonant changes that he thinks are harder to justify from Akkadian into West Semitic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=4957" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:37</a> | What books, tools, and sources does he recommend for going deep into ancient chronology? | He points to source texts and specialized chronological work, while noting that the field is difficult and needs careful method. | He says the field lacks a good up-to-date textbook and that chronology has changed rapidly because hand-built chronologies are too error-prone. Serious work now requires software, large numbers of calculations, and careful validation. For sources, he points especially to Mesopotamian Chronicles, eponym lists, Mari letters, Amarna letters, synchronisms in journals, and the need for a sourcebook comparable to Context of Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=5355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:15</a> | How does he understand Esther 2:5-7 and the question of who was taken captive? | He reads the grammar as referring to Mordecai's family line rather than requiring Mordecai himself to be impossibly old. | He says Mordecai is in the Persian period and was probably born in Babylon, perhaps around his sixties in the story, so the exile reference likely points to someone in his line rather than Mordecai himself. He does not force a firm identification, saying it was probably his father or grandfather and that he would need more study to decide between the options. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=5559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:39</a> | What does he think of the claim that Christ's life, not his death, is what saves, using Romans 5 and 1 Corinthians 15? | Based on the quoted summary, he rejects it as proof-texting and says Christ's death is central to salvation. | He reads Romans 5 in context as teaching reconciliation through the death and blood of Christ, then distinguishes that from being preserved by Christ's life after reconciliation. He also says 1 Corinthians 15 is about crucifixion and resurrection rather than a denial that Christ's death saves, so the quoted claim appears to him to be a serious theological error. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=6196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:16</a> | Were randomized methods considered legitimate for inquiring of the Lord in Proverbs 16:33, and are they still valid? | He explains the lot as a biblical practice but does not treat casual modern randomization as a simple equivalent. | He says ancient Israelites did use lots, Urim-like devices, and other determined signs in some biblical contexts, including the choice of Matthias and Gideon's fleece. But he treats those as a pre-Pentecost substitute for direct guidance, arguing that believers now have the Holy Spirit's instruction and should not use random methods as though they were still the normal way to inquire of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=6481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:01</a> | What view does he take on Pentateuchal authorship? | He favors a conservative supplementary-style approach over more radical source fragmentation. | He describes a conservative supplementary hypothesis: Moses produced core documents, later handed to Joshua and subsequent editors who connected, updated, and lightly supplemented them. He distinguishes this from liberal forms that resemble the documentary hypothesis, and he points to clear non-Mosaic elements such as Moses' death, post-conquest comments, and editorial glosses as evidence for later shaping without abandoning Mosaic core material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=6633" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:33</a> | Are there books or scholars that defend the supplementary view he described? | He points to peer-reviewed work and says the view appears more often in European scholarship. | He says the view is found more often in European scholarship than in American scholarship, which he says remains more attached to the documentary hypothesis. He specifically names Benjamin Kilchor as someone who has done peer-reviewed work in this area, while saying he does not know whether Kilchor has written a book-length defense of it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=6720" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:00</a> | Is the Bible better understood primarily as poetic work rather than straightforward story? | He says poetry matters deeply, but he would not push that framing so far that the Bible's historical claims disappear. | He says Brent Strawn has a point because poetry is compressed language and the Bible has deep poetic elements, but he resists reducing the Bible to either poetry or story alone. He stresses that history can be story, poetry can be true, and some sections should still be read in a historical-grammatical and even literal sense, so the usefulness of the claim depends on exactly what is meant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=6870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:30</a> | Can he give an in-depth analysis of Zoroastrianism? | He says he is not an expert and gives only a limited summary. | He identifies Zoroastrianism as a Persian religion and describes it as a theological dualism involving a good god of light and an evil god of darkness. He also mentions, with caveats, its unusual funerary practice of exposing bodies before collecting the bones, but repeatedly warns that this is not an area he has studied deeply. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=7022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:02</a> | Was God killing David's son a case of the child paying for the father's sin, and is that against the rules? | He distinguishes human judicial law from divine covenant judgment and consequences. | He says the Torah principle about children not paying for fathers' sins belongs to ordinary jurisprudence, not every form of divine judgment under the old covenant. In David's case, he says the child may not be personally guilty, and God may be removing protection or allowing death in a world with high infant mortality, but the event still functions as judgment and consequence for David's sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=7297" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:37</a> | Who is Pharaoh Neferneferuaten? | He says the figure is controversial and may be Nefertiti or one of Akhenaten's daughters. | He places Neferneferuaten after Smenkhkare and before Tutankhamun, with a reign of no more than about two years. Because Neferneferuaten is a throne name, he says the given name is uncertain; possibilities include Nefertiti, one of Akhenaten's daughters, or even a more complicated co-regency proposal, but he remains cautious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=7524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:24</a> | Does he use Logos Bible Software? | No. He says he uses BibleWorks and physical books instead. | He says he does not need Logos for his work because he uses BibleWorks when he needs searchable electronic Bible texts. For most other resources, he relies on a large physical book collection and hard copies rather than an electronic library platform. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=7678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:58</a> | What can Christians do to combat anti-Semitism in the church? | He says Christians should remember Romans, reject supersessionist hostility, and support Jewish people rightly. | He points first to Romans: God is not finished with the Jews, and Gentile Christians are grafted onto Israel's tree, not replacing it. He urges Christians to reject supersessionism, remember the Jewish roots of the faith and Jesus' identity as a Jew, call out anti-Semitism when they see it, stay anchored in church community rather than radicalized isolation, and support Messianic Jewish ministries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=8088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:48</a> | How does he identify Larsa and King Ariok in Genesis 14? | He says the identification is possible but uncertain and would need more study. | He says the place could be Larsa, but he does not commit without checking the relevant king lists and doing more etymological work. Even if the location is Larsa, he warns that Ariok might not appear as an independent ruler because he could have been a puppet governor or puppet king under Hammurabi's occupation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=8321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:41</a> | Does Christ still have a body in heaven? | Yes. He says Christ remains the resurrected God-man. | He says Christ rose bodily, showed Thomas his wounds, and remains the resurrected Lord; if he no longer had a body, he would no longer be resurrected in that sense. He also argues that embodied presence in heaven is not impossible, pointing to figures such as Enoch, Elijah, and Ezekiel's heavenly vision as examples that challenge a purely disembodied picture of heaven. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=8507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:47</a> | Are the Levitical sacrifices penal atonement, and how can flour be a substitute in Leviticus 5:5-11? | He says not all Levitical sacrifices are penal atonement, and blood atonement is broader than penal substitution. | He distinguishes different sacrifice types and says Leviticus 5 concerns trespass or ritual overstepping rather than high-handed rebellion. The offering covers the trespass but is not penal substitution in his terms; penal substitution can be a form of blood atonement, but not every blood atonement or sacrifice functions that way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=8779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:19</a> | What is known about Punt in the 14th century BC, especially its ruler, religion, and architecture? | He says the evidence is limited and comes mostly from Hatshepsut's Deir el-Bahri reliefs. | He says little is known about Punt beyond Egyptian evidence, especially the Hatshepsut expedition scenes. Those reliefs suggest a ruler or queen and show trade goods, but he says Punt's religion and architecture are largely unknown. The better-attested material concerns traded products such as gold, incense, animals, malachite, and the Egyptian exchange of bronze tools. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=9056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:56</a> | How do Old Testament apostasy laws differ from Islamic penalties for leaving Islam? | He says the systems are different and that ancient Israel usually did not execute someone merely for becoming apostate. | He says individual Israelites commonly fell into Baal worship without ordinary execution, though false prophecy, organized idolatrous towns, or evangelizing for other gods could bring severe penalties. He contrasts that with Islamic apostasy law, arguing that Old Testament penalties often function as maximum penalties and that apostasy was treated more like a spreading covenant disease than as an automatic death sentence for private unbelief. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=9463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:43</a> | For a book set in the same period, what did Egyptians call Greeks? | He thinks the term was Keftiu or a similar form, but he would want to verify it. | He answers from memory and says he believes the Egyptian term is Keftiu, while checking a dictionary without finding the confirmation quickly during the stream. He leaves the answer tentative and offers to verify it later, so the useful takeaway is the likely term plus his caveat that it needed confirmation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=9929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:29</a> | Why do Egyptian loanwords drop off so sharply after the Exodus materials? | He says the shift reflects changing language environment and setting once Israel is no longer embedded in Egypt. | He explains that the Israelites' language would shift during the forty years in the wilderness as contact with Egyptians decreased and contact with West Semitic groups increased. Early Exodus material would naturally preserve more Egyptian influence, but by the end of the wilderness period Israel would be increasingly de-Egyptianized and linguistically more West Semitic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=10175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:35</a> | Can anachronisms help date the approximate time when the Pentateuch was finished in the form we have? | He says some can help, but many alleged anachronisms are better described as retrojections. | He distinguishes true anachronism from retrojective naming, where later terms are used for reader clarity. Examples such as "Pharaoh," "Ramses," and references to Philistines can still help narrow stages of composition or updating, but he treats them as literary and historical markers that must be handled carefully rather than as simple proof of late fabrication. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=10375" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:55</a> | If Genesis 1:1 is a title, would adding a colon make that clearer in English? | He says maybe for English readers, but he does not encourage idiosyncratic punctuation. | He thinks a colon might clarify the title reading for some English readers, but he warns that unusual punctuation can confuse highly literate audiences and make the text look awkward. He uses the NASB as an example of a translation whose punctuation tries to expose underlying grammar but can make the English harder to read. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=10784" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:44</a> | What does he think of the Septuagint versus the Masoretic Text? | He prefers the Masoretic Text as a reading edition but uses text criticism case by case, including the Septuagint and other witnesses. | He prefers the Masoretic Text for regular reading, but he uses the Septuagint, Samaritan Pentateuch, Dead Sea Scrolls, and other witnesses in text-critical work. He notes that the apostles used the Septuagint and that it is based on an old textual tradition, but because it is also a translation that can be wrong, each variant has to be judged case by case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=11548" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:28</a> | What does he think about Trinitarian theology, modalism, divine simplicity, and the filioque? | He rejects modalism as non-Trinitarian and says he does not support the filioque. | He says modalism collapses the persons of the Trinity into contextual switching and is not true Trinitarianism. He identifies with an Athanasian-style affirmation that God was always Triune, rejects eternal generation as unnecessary, and therefore also rejects the filioque because he does not accept the premise behind it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=12075" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:15</a> | Who has the worse Egyptian chronology, David Rohl or Roger Pearlman? | He says he cannot assess Pearlman, but he can say Rohl's chronology is very bad. | He says he does not know Pearlman's chronology well enough to comment, but he strongly criticizes Rohl's chronology and also calls David Down's worse. He says Rohl's chronology has been tested in Groundhog in multiple iterations and shows severe internal inconsistency, with dozens of conflicts that make it effectively impossible as a chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=12856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:16</a> | Could a similar chronological system be used to date events in the Bible? | Yes, but it would need to be adapted carefully for clans and biblical genealogical data. | He says the same kind of data-driven chronological system could be used, but biblical data would require adaptation for clans and genealogical structures. He flags honorific transposition and non-sequential genealogical material as major complications, and says competent chronology requires enough evidence and internal consistency rather than simply assigning dates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=13111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:31</a> | Why did Moses break the Ten Commandment tablets? | He says the act symbolized that the covenant had already been broken before it was even formally ratified. | He calls the tablet-breaking a parabolic act: Moses sees the golden calf and shows Israel that the covenant is broken before full ratification. He rejects the idea that this was simply how ancient treaties were normally voided, explaining that suzerain treaties usually activated curse terms when broken; here Moses' act uniquely dramatizes Israel's rebellion, though Moses' anger also becomes part of the episode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=13891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:31</a> | Did the three-fish painting behind him come with the apartment, or is it his own work? | He says it is three poppies, not three fish, and it is not presented as his major subject. | He clarifies that the painting shows three poppies, not three fish, and that it was painted by his producer rather than by him. It also did not come with the apartment; he says they enjoy having it up and can understand why someone might mistake the image for fish. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=14131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:31</a> | Does a beginner need preparatory study before reading his Ark of the Covenant book? | No. The book was written for a lay audience rather than only for specialists. | He says no preparatory course is needed because the book was written to be approachable for a lay audience, with humor and many pictures. He describes it as bridging academic and lay readers: some can read it quickly, others can study details slowly, and even readers without much biblical background can still benefit from the illustrations and presentation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Ou2jBECn4rM?t=14541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:21</a> | Is his Ark of the Covenant book available on Kindle? | He does not think there is a Kindle edition, but he says a Logos edition exists and recommends the hardcover. | He says he has not seen a Kindle edition and, after checking, still thinks there is not one. He knows of a Logos Bible Software electronic edition, but because the book is photograph-heavy and meant to be visually beautiful, he recommends the hardcover over electronic formats even though he may receive more from electronic royalties. |
